Q1: Honestly, my study routine wasn't super structured in terms of logging into the LMS constantly. I'd dedicate solid chunks of time, maybe 23 hours a week, but a lot of that was me reading through printed notes or working through materials I'd already downloaded. I'm a read-write learner, so I'd often transcribe things by hand or type up summaries. The LMS was more of a starting point than my main workspace.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I'm involved in extracurriculars, so some weeks I'd have to cram my studying into fewer but longer sessions. When deadlines approached, I'd get more focused, but otherwise I spread things out based on what felt manageable that week.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check an assignment deadline or download new materials. Sometimes I'd pop in to see if there were discussion posts worth reading or if the instructor had posted announcements. But honestly, once I had what I needed, I'd log out pretty quickly and work offline.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a daily habit for me. I'd check it a few times a week at most, usually at the start of the week to plan and then again before something was due. My routine was more about the actual studying than the platform itself.
Q5: The reading materials and assignment briefs were the most useful to me. I learn best by reading and writing, so having clear written instructions and PDFs I could download and annotate was perfect. The discussion posts were occasionally helpful for seeing different perspectives, but I relied heavily on the written content.
Q6: I mostly ignored the video content, to be honest. I watched some of it, but sitting through videos isn't how I learn best. I'd rather read a transcript or summary. Without reliable internet access at home, streaming videos was also just impractical, so I'd skip them unless absolutely necessary.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what I'd be assessed on or if it helped clarify something I was confused about. The gamified tasks they introduced actually made some things more engaging for me—there was something satisfying about completing challenges and seeing progress, even if my motivation was generally low.
Q8: Optional activities felt like extras I could skip if I was pressed for time. Required ones got my attention because they affected my grade. But sometimes an optional activity, especially the gamified ones, would catch my interest and I'd do it anyway. It depended on how stressed I was feeling that week.
Q9: Absolutely. Most of my studying happened offline with printed materials, handwritten notes, and textbooks I borrowed from the library. I'd download everything I could when I had internet access and then work through it at home without being connected. My LMS activity doesn't reflect even half of what I actually did.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my notes, rewriting key concepts, or working through practice problems on paper. None of that shows up anywhere in the system. I could study for an entire weekend and the LMS would show nothing because I wasn't logged in—I was just working with what I'd already saved.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a reading or download a PDF once, and then that would be my study material for days or even weeks. I'd print things out, highlight them, make margin notes. The single download doesn't capture the hours I spent actually engaging with that content afterward.
Q12: There were definitely moments when stress piled up—my stress levels have been pretty high this semester. When extracurriculars got demanding or personal stuff came up, it was harder to muster the energy to engage. But I'm pretty resilient, so I'd push through even when I didn't feel like it.
Q13: Fatigue and competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS use. Sometimes I'd be too tired to log in and would just work from my printed notes instead. Without consistent internet, logging in felt like an extra step I'd skip when I was already overwhelmed. I'd prioritize the actual studying over the platform.
Q14: Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts or skim materials without really absorbing anything. I'd be logged in but my mind was elsewhere, especially during high-stress periods. Those sessions felt unproductive even though technically I was 'active' on the system.
Q15: I definitely postponed some assignments even when I knew they mattered. My motivation has been low this semester, so sometimes I'd just need a mental break. I'd tell myself I'd get to it later, and usually I did, but the procrastination was real. The gamified elements actually helped me push through some of that.
Q16: Deadlines were my main driver for LMS activity. I'd log in more frequently as due dates approached, submit my work, and then my activity would drop again. The system probably shows spikes around deadlines and quiet periods in between, which accurately reflects how I operated.
Q17: Feedback motivated me to keep going, especially when it was positive. Getting good scores on assignments reminded me that my approach was working, even if I wasn't super active online. When feedback was constructive, I'd adjust my studying, but I processed that feedback offline in my notes.
Q18: The course structure gave me flexibility, which I needed given my extracurriculars and limited internet. Having materials available to download meant I could work on my own schedule. But the structure also meant I could get away with minimal LMS engagement as long as I submitted quality work.
Q19: Having downloadable readings made engagement easier for someone like me without consistent internet. What made it harder was when things required real-time participation or video watching—that just didn't fit my situation. The gamified tasks were a nice touch that made some activities more appealing.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for answers, exploring out of curiosity, or just feeling anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually completing work. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. They might be struggling to understand what's expected or feeling overwhelmed.
Q21: That's basically me. My LMS activity looks minimal, but I performed well because I studied extensively offline. I downloaded materials, worked through them thoroughly on paper, and only logged in when necessary. The system doesn't capture the depth of engagement that happens away from the screen.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone accessed materials and submitted work, which is useful for tracking deadlines. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement, and whether someone actually understood the material. They measure clicks, not comprehension.
Q23: Being engaged means actually understanding and caring about what you're learning, not just going through the motions. For me, it's about deep reading, taking meaningful notes, and connecting ideas. It's a mental investment that doesn't require being logged into anything.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent with printed readings, the handwritten notes I made, or the genuine interest I developed in certain topics. It wouldn't capture my resilience in pushing through stress or how the gamified tasks actually boosted my mood. My real engagement happened mostly offline.
